post.description:
Creates a new user account in the system. The user provides their email, password, first name,
and last name. A verification OTP is automatically sent to the provided email address for account
activation. The password is securely hashed before storage.

 ACCESS: Public (anyone can signup)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Email must be unique in the system
   - Password is automatically hashed for security
   - Verification OTP is sent to email immediately
   - User must verify email before they can login

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a new user wants to create an account to access the platform

Resends the verification OTP to a user's email address. This is useful when the original
OTP has expired or was not received. A new OTP is generated and sent to the registered
email address.

 ACCESS: Public (no authentication required)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Can be called multiple times for the same user
   - Previous OTP becomes invalid when new one is generated
   - User must be registered (email must exist in system)
   - OTP has an expiration time

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a user didn't receive their verification email or the OTP expired before they could use it

Verifies the OTP (One-Time Password) sent to a user's email and activates their account.
Once verified, the user's account is marked as active and they can proceed to login.

 ACCESS: Public (no authentication required)

 KEY POINTS:
   - OTP must match the one sent to the user's email
   - OTP must not be expired
   - Account is automatically activated upon successful verification
   - OTP can only be used once

 WHEN TO USE:
   After receiving the OTP via email during signup or when resending verification

Authenticates a user with their email and password. Returns an access token (JWT) that must
be included in the Authorization header for subsequent authenticated requests. The user's
account must be verified before they can login.

 ACCESS: Public (no authentication required, but user must be verified)

 KEY POINTS:
   - User must have verified their email with OTP first
   - Password is compared against the hashed version in database
   - Returns JWT access token for authenticated requests
   - Token should be sent as "Bearer <token>" in Authorization header

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a verified user wants to access protected features of the platform

Logs out the current user by invalidating their access token. The token is added to a
blacklist and can no longer be used for authenticated requests. User must login again
to get a new valid token.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (requires valid token in Authorization header)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Token is immediately invalidated and added to blacklist
   - User must provide Authorization header with their current token
   - After logout, all requests with that token will be rejected
   - User needs to login again to get a new valid token

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a user wants to securely end their session and invalidate their access token

Initiates the password reset process for a user who has forgotten their password. Sends
a password reset OTP to the user's registered email address. This OTP is required to
complete the password reset via the reset-password endpoint.

 ACCESS: Public (no authentication required)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Email must be registered in the system
   - OTP is sent to the registered email address
   - OTP has an expiration time for security
   - Does not reveal whether email exists (security measure)

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a user cannot remember their password and needs to reset it

Completes the password reset process by verifying the OTP and setting a new password.
This is the second step after calling forgot-password. The user provides the OTP received
via email along with their new password.

 ACCESS: Public (no authentication required, but requires valid OTP)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Requires valid OTP from forgot-password endpoint
   - OTP must not be expired
   - New password is securely hashed before storage
   - OTP is invalidated after successful password reset
   - User can login with new password immediately

 WHEN TO USE:
   After receiving the password reset OTP via email from the forgot-password process

Allows a logged-in user to change their password. User must provide their current password
for verification before setting a new password. This is different from reset-password which
is for users who have forgotten their password.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (requires authentication)

 KEY POINTS:
   - User must provide correct current password
   - New password is securely hashed before storage
   - User remains logged in after password change
   - Current password verification prevents unauthorized changes

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a logged-in user wants to change their password for security reasons or preference

Creates a brand new cloud account within the specified cloud provider's organization
structure. This provisions a new account at the cloud provider level and registers it
in the platform. The account is automatically linked to the user's organization for the
specified provider.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Creates a NEW account at the cloud provider (AWS, Huawei, Azure, etc.)
   - Requires valid cloud provider credentials configured
   - Account is automatically linked to user's organization
   - User becomes the admin of the newly created account
   - Query parameter 'csp' specifies the cloud provider (e.g., ?csp=aws or ?csp=huawei)
   - Account creation may take several minutes at provider level

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to provision a completely new cloud account (not joining an existing one)
   under your organization's management

Initiates the process of inviting an existing cloud provider account to join your organization.
This is STEP 1 of a 2-step workflow. The endpoint sends an invitation to the target account
through the cloud provider's organization service and returns a handshake_id for tracking.
After the account owner accepts the invitation in their cloud provider console, you must
call the complete_invite_account endpoint to finalize.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - This is STEP 1 of a 2-step process
   - ONLY sends the invitation - does NOT create organizations or OUs
   - Returns handshake_id which must be saved for step 2
   - Account owner must accept invitation in their cloud provider console (external action)
   - Query parameter 'csp' specifies cloud provider (e.g., ?csp=aws or ?csp=huawei)
   - Must call /complete_invite_account after acceptance to finish the process
   - Invitation may expire if not accepted within provider's time limit

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to bring an existing cloud account under your organization's management
   structure instead of creating a new account from scratch

Completes the account invitation process after the account owner has accepted the invitation
in their cloud provider console. This is STEP 2 of a 2-step workflow. Creates or retrieves
the organization and organizational unit (OU), finalizes the invitation workflow at the provider
level, and registers the account in the platform database.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - This is STEP 2 - must call initiate_invite_account first
   - Requires handshake_id from the initiate_invite_account response
   - Account owner must have accepted invitation in cloud provider console
   - Creates/retrieves organization and OU at provider level
   - Finalizes invitation workflow and saves account to database
   - Query parameter 'csp' specifies cloud provider (e.g., ?csp=aws or ?csp=huawei)
   - Account becomes accessible in platform after successful completion

 WHEN TO USE:
   After the account owner accepts your invitation in their cloud console, use this to
   complete the onboarding and make the account available in the platform

Grants or ensures admin-level access to a cloud account by creating or verifying the
necessary IAM roles and permissions at the cloud provider level. This enables the platform
to manage the account on behalf of the user.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must have admin rights to the account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Creates or verifies admin IAM roles in the cloud account
   - Enables platform to manage the account on user's behalf
   - Query parameter 'csp' specifies cloud provider (e.g., ?csp=aws or ?csp=huawei)
   - Required for certain automated management operations
   - Idempotent - safe to call multiple times
   - User must have sufficient permissions in the cloud account

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to enable platform management capabilities for an account or troubleshoot
   permission issues

Generates a direct console login URL for a cloud account and sends it via email to the
current user. This provides quick access to the account's management console at the cloud
provider without needing to manually navigate through the provider's portal. Works for
multiple cloud providers (AWS, Huawei, Azure, etc.).

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be member of the account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Generates temporary console login URL for the cloud account
   - URL is sent to user's registered email address
   - Works for multiple providers (AWS, Huawei, Azure, etc.)
   - Query parameter 'csp' specifies cloud provider (e.g., ?csp=aws or ?csp=huawei)
   - URL may have limited validity time based on provider policies
   - Provides direct access to account management console

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need quick access to a cloud account's management console without manually
   navigating the provider's portal

get.description:
Searches for cloud accounts across all providers by matching against account name, email,
or provider-specific account ID. Returns a list of matching accounts with optional filtering
to include or exclude inactive accounts.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Search query must be at least 1 character long
   - Searches across account name, email, and provider ID fields
   - By default only returns active accounts
   - Set include_inactive=true to see closed/suspended accounts
   - Returns accounts from all cloud providers (AWS, Huawei, Azure, etc.)

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to find specific accounts across your organization by name, email, or ID

parameters.name: q

schema.description: Search query for account name, email, or provider ID
schema.title: Q

parameters.description: Search query for account name, email, or provider ID

parameters.name: include_inactive

schema.description: Include inactive accounts in search results
schema.title: Include Inactive

parameters.description: Include inactive accounts in search results

get.description:
Retrieves all cloud accounts that the currently logged-in user is a member of, regardless
of their role (admin or regular member). Returns comprehensive account details including
provider information, account status, and the user's role within each account.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Shows all accounts where user has any level of membership
   - Includes accounts from all cloud providers (AWS, Huawei, Azure, etc.)
   - Returns both active and inactive accounts
   - Displays user's role (Admin or Member) for each account

 WHEN TO USE:
   When a user wants to see all the cloud accounts they have access to across all providers

Retrieves detailed information about a specific cloud account by its unique ID. Returns
comprehensive account details including provider information, status, organization membership,
and account metadata.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Returns account regardless of status (active or inactive)
   - Includes provider-specific account details
   - Shows organization and member information
   - Account ID must be valid UUID format

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need detailed information about a specific account, including inactive ones

put.description:
Updates account details such as account name, email, or other metadata. This is a
privileged operation that can only be performed by users who are administrators
of the specific account being updated.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can update account details
   - Cannot update provider-specific fields (e.g., provider account ID)
   - Account status updates use separate endpoint
   - Changes are immediately reflected in the system

 WHEN TO USE:
   When an account admin needs to update account information like name or contact details

get.description:
Retrieves detailed information about a specific cloud account by its ID, but ONLY if the
account is currently active. This endpoint will fail if the account is closed, suspended,
or inactive, providing a safety check for operations that should only work on active accounts.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only returns accounts with active status
   - Throws error if account is closed, suspended, or inactive
   - Useful for operations that require active accounts
   - Safer than general get endpoint for sensitive operations

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to ensure an account is active before performing operations on it

patch.description:
Updates the membership type/role of a specific member within an account. Can promote
members to admin or demote admins to regular members. Note that only one admin is
allowed per account, so promoting someone to admin requires careful coordination.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can update member roles
   - Account can only have one admin at a time
   - Use transfer_admin endpoint for safer admin role transfers
   - Target user must already be a member of the account
   - Cannot modify your own role

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to change a member's role (e.g., promote a trusted member to admin)

post.description:
Transfers the admin role from the current admin to another existing member of the account.
This is an atomic operation that demotes the current admin to a regular member while
simultaneously promoting the target user to admin, ensuring the account always has
exactly one admin at all times.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be current admin of the account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only the current admin can transfer their role
   - Target user must already be a member of the account
   - Operation is atomic - both role changes happen together
   - Cannot transfer to yourself or to a non-existent user
   - Account always maintains exactly one admin

 WHEN TO USE:
   When an admin wants to hand over account management responsibility to another trusted
   team member (e.g., during role changes, offboarding, or delegation)

patch.description:
Updates the status of a cloud account (e.g., active, suspended, closed). This controls
the account's operational state and access permissions. Status changes affect whether
users can access and perform operations on the account.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can change account status
   - Valid statuses typically include: active, suspended, closed
   - Status changes are logged for audit purposes
   - Use specific endpoints (close, reactivate) for common operations

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to change an account's operational status for administrative reasons

post.description:
Closes (soft deletes) a cloud account by marking it as inactive. This prevents further
operations on the account while preserving the account data for audit and historical
purposes. The account can be reactivated later if needed.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - This is a soft delete - data is not permanently removed
   - Account becomes inaccessible to members after closing
   - Account can be reactivated using the reactivate endpoint
   - Historical data and audit logs are preserved
   - Does not affect the actual cloud provider account

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to deactivate an account that is no longer needed but preserve its data

Reactivates a previously closed cloud account by restoring its active status. This makes
the account accessible again to its members and allows normal operations to resume. All
previous account data and member relationships are restored.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Account must have been previously closed
   - All member relationships are restored
   - Account data and history remain intact
   - Members can immediately access the account again
   - Previous configurations are preserved

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to restore access to a closed account that is needed again

get.description:
Retrieves a complete list of all members who have access to a specific cloud account.
Returns detailed information about each member including their name, email, role
(Admin or Member), and membership status.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be member of the account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Returns all members with access to the account
   - Includes member role (Admin or Member)
   - Shows member status and join date
   - Typically one admin and multiple regular members
   - User must be a member of the account to view members list

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to see who has access to an account and their roles/permissions

delete.description:
Removes a member from a cloud account by deleting their membership relationship. The
removed user will lose all access to the account immediately. This does not delete
the user's account from the system, only their membership in this specific cloud account.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can remove members
   - Cannot remove the account's admin (must transfer admin first)
   - User immediately loses access to the account
   - User's account in the system is not deleted
   - Can re-add the user later via invitation

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to revoke a team member's access to a cloud account (e.g., employee departure,
   role change, or security concerns)

get.description:
Retrieves all organizations owned by the current user. Each user can have one organization
per cloud provider (e.g., one for AWS, one for Huawei, one for Azure). Organizations act
as containers for managing multiple cloud accounts under a single management structure.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Returns all organizations where user is the owner
   - Each user has one organization per cloud provider
   - Organizations group multiple accounts together
   - Automatically created when user creates their first account with a provider
   - Contains provider-specific organization details and IDs

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to see all your cloud provider organizations and their associated accounts

Retrieves detailed information about a specific organization by its unique ID. Returns
organization metadata including provider information, organization name, provider-specific
organization ID, and associated account counts.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Returns comprehensive organization details
   - Includes provider-specific organization IDs
   - Shows owner information
   - Displays account count and relationships
   - Organization ID must be valid UUID format

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need detailed information about a specific organization and its configuration

put.description:
Updates organization details such as organization name or other metadata. This allows
customizing the organization information displayed in the platform. Note that provider-specific
organization IDs cannot be modified as they are managed by the cloud provider.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be organization owner)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only organization owner can update organization details
   - Cannot modify provider-specific organization IDs
   - Changes are immediately reflected in the system
   - Organization name and metadata can be customized
   - Does not affect actual cloud provider organization

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to update your organization's display name or other platform-specific metadata

get.description:
Retrieves all cloud accounts belonging to the current user's organization for a specific
cloud provider. Automatically finds the user's organization for the specified provider
and returns all accounts within that organization.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Filters accounts by cloud provider (aws, huawei, azure, etc.)
   - Automatically finds user's organization for the specified provider
   - Returns all accounts within that organization
   - Includes account details, status, and membership info
   - Provider name is case-insensitive

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to see all your accounts for a specific cloud provider (e.g., all AWS accounts
   or all Huawei accounts)

post.description:
Adds an existing cloud account to an organization. This links the account to the organization's
management structure, allowing it to be managed alongside other accounts in the same organization.
The account must already exist in the system.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be organization owner)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Account must already exist in the system
   - Account must belong to the same cloud provider as the organization
   - Links account to organization's management structure
   - Account becomes visible when querying organization accounts
   - Does not affect actual cloud provider organization structure

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you have an existing account that needs to be organized under your organization's
   management structure

Removes an account from an organization by soft-deleting the relationship. The account
is no longer managed under this organization but the account data is preserved. This
does not delete the actual cloud account, only its association with the organization.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be organization owner)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Soft delete - account data is preserved
   - Removes account from organization's management structure
   - Account no longer appears in organization's account list
   - Does not affect the actual cloud provider account
   - Can be re-added to organization if needed later

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to remove an account from your organization's management without deleting
   the account itself

Creates and sends an invitation for a user to join a cloud account. The invitation is
sent via email to the recipient, who can then accept or reject it. Only account admins
can create invitations, and the invitation grants access to the specified account with
the designated role.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can send invitations
   - Invitation is sent via email to the recipient
   - Recipient must have an account in the system or will need to sign up
   - Invitation has an expiration time
   - Can specify role (Admin or Member) for the new member
   - Invitation can be revoked before acceptance

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to grant another user access to your cloud account as a team member

Accepts an invitation to join a cloud account and grants the user immediate access with
the role specified in the invitation. After acceptance, the user becomes a member of the
account and can perform operations according to their assigned role.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be the invitation recipient)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Invitation must be addressed to the current user's email
   - Invitation must not be expired, revoked, or already processed
   - User gains immediate access to the account upon acceptance
   - User's role (Admin or Member) is determined by the invitation
   - Invitation becomes invalid after acceptance

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you receive an invitation email and want to accept access to a cloud account

Rejects an invitation to join a cloud account. The user will not gain access to the
account, and the invitation is marked as rejected. The sender can see that their
invitation was declined.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users (must be the invitation recipient)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Invitation must be addressed to the current user
   - User does not gain access to the account
   - Invitation is permanently marked as rejected
   - Sender can see the rejection status
   - Cannot reject an already processed invitation

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you receive an invitation but do not want to join the cloud account

Revokes a previously sent invitation before it can be accepted. The invitation becomes
invalid and cannot be used by the recipient. This is useful when an invitation was sent
in error or the recipient should no longer receive access.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can revoke invitations
   - Can only revoke pending invitations (not already accepted/rejected)
   - Recipient can no longer accept the revoked invitation
   - Useful for correcting mistakes or changing access plans
   - Invitation is permanently marked as revoked

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to cancel a pending invitation before the recipient accepts it (e.g., sent
   to wrong email, policy change, or no longer need the person on the team)

get.description:
Retrieves all invitations that have been sent to the current user's email address.
Shows invitations to join various cloud accounts, with optional filtering by invitation
status (pending, accepted, rejected, etc.).

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Shows all invitations addressed to the current user
   - Can filter by status: PENDING, ACCEPTED, REJECTED, REVOKED, EXPIRED
   - Includes invitation details: sender, account, role, expiration
   - Pending invitations can be accepted or rejected
   - Historical record of all received invitations

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to see all pending invitations waiting for your response or review your
   invitation history

parameters.name: status

schema.description: Filter by status: PENDING, ACCEPTED, REJECTED, REVOKED, or EXPIRED
schema.title: Status

parameters.description: Filter by status: PENDING, ACCEPTED, REJECTED, REVOKED, or EXPIRED

get.description:
Retrieves all invitations that have been sent for a specific cloud account. Shows who
has been invited, their status (pending, accepted, rejected), and invitation details.
Only account admins can view this information.

 ACCESS: Admin only (must be admin of the specific account)

 KEY POINTS:
   - Only account admins can view account invitations
   - Shows all invitations sent for the account
   - Can filter by status: PENDING, ACCEPTED, REJECTED, REVOKED, EXPIRED
   - Includes recipient details and invitation metadata
   - Useful for tracking who has been invited and their response

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you want to see all pending invitations for your account or track invitation history
   and responses

parameters.name: status

schema.description: Filter by status: PENDING, ACCEPTED, REJECTED, REVOKED, or EXPIRED
schema.title: Status

parameters.description: Filter by status: PENDING, ACCEPTED, REJECTED, REVOKED, or EXPIRED

get.description:
Retrieves all pending cloud provider account invitations for the current user. Returns
handshake IDs representing active invitation workflows at the cloud provider level
(AWS Organizations, Huawei Organizations, etc.). These are tracked during the multi-step
account invitation process.

 ACCESS: Logged-in users

 KEY POINTS:
   - Returns handshake IDs from cloud provider invitation workflows
   - These are provider-level invitations (AWS, Huawei, etc.)
   - Handshake IDs are added when initiating account invitations
   - Handshake IDs are removed when invitations are completed or fail
   - Used to track the status of ongoing cloud account invitation processes
   - Different from internal platform invitations

 WHEN TO USE:
   When you need to check which cloud provider account invitations are still pending and
   need to be completed (Step 2 of the invite workflow)
